summary: Replace inline webhook delivery with a durable async retry queue: persist outbound messages, a worker drains the queue with exponential backoff up to 24 hours, terminal failures land in a dead-letter with an operator alert. At-least-once guarantee, restart-survival, and a staged enterprise rollout behind a flag.

reversibility.why: The feature flag gates the cutover so a rollback path exists, but once messages are durable the schema and retry bookkeeping are hard to revert without data loss.

blast_radius.why: Every outbound webhook flows through the new path once enabled; a retry storm or DLQ outage hits every subscribed tenant.

compliance_scope.why: Webhook payloads may carry tenant data but there's no new regulated surface — this is a delivery-mechanism change.

user_facing_impact.why: Enterprise subscribers see changed delivery semantics (eventually-delivered vs. dropped); developer UX unchanged.

novelty.why: Durable retry queues are well-known patterns but this is the first one in this codebase; idempotency + DLQ semantics are new surface area.

scope_effort.why: New persistence table, worker loop, retry policy, DLQ, alerting, gateway flag — several components but each is small.

state_complexity.why: Durable outbox + at-least-once delivery + 24h backoff state = real persistence discipline; ordering and dedup must be explicit.

operational_risk.why: Retry storms on a broken subscriber, thundering herd after a bulk enqueue, DLQ growth — all production-runtime behaviors that need guard rails.

coordination_cost.why: Backend + SRE + release-engineering need to agree on rollout + alerting; not cross-org but not a solo effort either.

specialists.name: solution-architect
specialists.why: Picks the persistence + worker topology and names the trade-offs (outbox table vs. queue service vs. sidecar).

specialists.name: backend-engineer
specialists.why: Implements the outbox, worker loop, retry policy, and DLQ.

specialists.name: test-strategist
specialists.why: Designs retry-policy and DLQ scenarios before code — happy path, 5xx burst, subscriber flap, 24h expiry.

specialists.name: sre
specialists.why: Owns alert thresholds, DLQ size SLO, retry-storm runbook, feature-flag rollout safety.

specialists.name: release-engineer
specialists.why: Runs the enterprise-10 canary behind the feature flag and coordinates revert.

phases.name: design
phases.why: Pick outbox shape, retry/backoff policy, and DLQ alert thresholds; document trade-offs.

phases.name: test-strategy
phases.why: Define retry + DLQ test cases and failure-injection plan BEFORE build, given state_complexity is high.

phases.name: build
phases.why: Implement outbox persistence, worker, retry policy, DLQ, feature flag, alert plumbing.

phases.name: test
phases.why: Execute unit + integration + failure-injection scenarios against a real Postgres + fake subscriber rig.

phases.name: review
phases.why: SRE + senior engineer sign off on retry semantics, DLQ alerting, and the rollout flag before enterprise-10 enablement.

rigor_why: State + operational risk are HIGH but there's a feature flag and a 10-account canary, so standard rigor with a mandatory test-strategy phase is appropriate; full rigor would demand compliance + security review that isn't warranted for a delivery mechanism change.
complexity_why: Durable state + at-least-once + DLQ + staged rollout puts this squarely in feature-with-coordination territory, a bit above mid-scale CRUD.
open_questions: What is the at-least-once dedup key on the subscriber side — message id or a caller-provided idempotency header?
Does the DLQ alert page the on-call or only post to a channel? SLO target for DLQ age?
Should the worker be per-tenant (throughput isolation) or global (simpler) on day one?

tasks.title: Write test strategy: retry, DLQ, subscriber-flap, 24h expiry scenarios
Implement worker loop + exponential backoff + DLQ handoff
Wire feature flag + enterprise-10 allowlist + DLQ alert
Execute retry + DLQ + subscriber-flap + perf-baseline scenarios
Review: retry semantics + alerting + rollout plan sign-off
